title: Your first generic function
instruction:
A generic function uses `<T>` as a type parameter filled in at call time. TypeScript infers `T` from the argument — no explicit annotation needed.

Write generic identity and first-element functions.
successMessage: `T` is inferred at each call: `identity(42)` infers `T=number`, `identity("hello")` infers `T=string`. The function works for any type without `any`.

title: Generic collections — typed containers
instruction:
Generic classes create reusable containers that work with any type while maintaining type safety.

Build a generic Queue data structure.
successMessage: `Queue<string>` ensures only strings can be enqueued, and `dequeue()` returns `string | undefined` — not `any`. TypeScript checks every operation.

title: Generic API response wrapper
instruction:
Wrap every API response in a generic type so callers know exactly what data type they'll receive.

Build a typed API client wrapper.
successMessage: `ApiResponse<T>` is the standard pattern for typed API clients. TypeScript knows `userResult.data` is `User | null` — forcing null checks. The same wrapper works for users, orders, and any other type.

title: Generic constraints — limit what T can be
instruction:
`T extends SomeType` restricts the allowed types. This lets you safely access properties guaranteed by the constraint.

Write functions that work only on specific shapes.
successMessage: `T extends HasId` guarantees `.id` exists. `T extends HasName` guarantees `.name` exists. Both functions work on any object with those fields — users, products, orders.

title: Multiple type parameters
instruction:
A generic function can have multiple independent type parameters, each inferred separately.

Build map, filter, and zip with typed generics.
successMessage: `mapArray<string, number>` is inferred from the callback — `s => s.length` means `T=string, U=number`. `zip<string, number>` is inferred from the arrays. Two independent type parameters, both correctly tracked.

title: Generic utility functions
instruction:
Generic functions make utility code reusable across all types. Here are common generic utilities used in real codebases.

Build a set of generic collection utilities.
successMessage: `groupBy` works for any type with any key type. `unique` works for any array. TypeScript infers the types from the arguments — no manual annotation at call sites.

title: Generic interfaces — reusable type shapes
instruction:
Interfaces can have type parameters, enabling reusable contracts that work across different data types.

Create a generic repository and event emitter interface.
successMessage: `EventEmitter<TEvents>` is typed to the specific events. `emitter.emit("login", { wrong: true })` would be a type error. TypeScript validates the payload type for each event.

title: Generic conditional defaults
instruction:
Generic functions can use conditional types to provide intelligent defaults based on what type is provided.

Build a generic optional value wrapper.
successMessage: `getOrDefault` infers `T` from the default value — `getOrDefault(null, 42)` infers `T=number`. `0` is kept because `??` only triggers on `null`/`undefined`, not `0` or `false`.

title: Variadic generics — tuple types
instruction:
Generics can capture the types of variadic arguments using tuple rest types. This enables precise typing for functions that work with argument lists.

Build a typed pipe function.
successMessage: Function overloads give `pipe` precise types for different arities. Each function's return type must match the next function's parameter type. TypeScript catches type mismatches in the pipeline at compile time.

title: Generic error handling — typed Result
instruction:
A generic `Result<T, E>` type encapsulates either a successful value or an error. Callers must handle both cases before using the value.

Build a typed Result type with chaining.
successMessage: `Result<T>` forces callers to check `.success` before accessing `.value`. This is the Rust-inspired error handling pattern — popular in TypeScript codebases that avoid throwing.

title: Generic higher-order functions
instruction:
Higher-order functions that create other functions can preserve full type safety using generics.

Build a generic memoize and retry wrapper.
successMessage: `memoize` preserves the exact type of the wrapped function. `memoFib` still expects a `number` and returns a `number`. The generic `TArgs` and `TReturn` capture the function's exact signature.

title: Generic constraints with keyof
instruction:
`keyof T` produces a union of all property names of `T`. Combined with generics, it enables type-safe property access.

Build type-safe object accessors.
successMessage: `K extends keyof T` constrains `key` to only valid property names of `T`. `getProperty(user, "invalid")` would be a compile error. `T[K]` is the value type at that key — no casting needed.
